intro: Hey, welcome back to The Weekly Rundown, where we've got drama, redemption, and a whole lot of chaos to catch you up on! From Jets fans finally getting put to work to Wimbledon royalty taking a shocking fall, this week did *not* come to play.

stories.headline: Jets Fans, Your Offseason Homework Is Due
stories.body: The Jets have been busy this offseason, and cornerback Nahshon Wright is apparently a fan of what the front office has been up to. One Jets undrafted free agent even cracked an All-UDFA team, which is either a great sign or a very low bar — probably both.

stories.headline: Oregon's Defense Is Already Getting Hyped for 2026
stories.body: ESPN dropped their top 10 college defensive players entering 2026, and the Ducks landed two names on the list — not bad for a program that's still settling into the Big Ten. If Washington and Finney can stay healthy and hungry, Oregon's defense could be a real headache for opposing offenses this fall.

stories.headline: Mizzou Is Betting Big on a Wide Receiver Named Cayden Lee
stories.body: Missouri is building out what could be a genuinely dangerous offense in 2026, and All-SEC wideout Cayden Lee is the piece they're counting on to tie it all together. If the Tigers can get consistent production at receiver, their offense might finally live up to the hype fans have been waiting on.

stories.headline: Maliq Brown Lands in San Antonio and Feels Right at Home
stories.body: The Spurs have a knack for making young players feel like they belong, and Maliq Brown is the latest to walk into that culture. Whether it's the coaching staff, the system, or just the Spurs magic, Brown seems to have landed in exactly the right spot to develop. Keep an eye on this one — San Antonio has a way of quietly turning prospects into players.

stories.headline: An NBA Vet Has Thoughts on How the WNBA Is Treating Caitlin Clark
stories.body: An 11-year NBA veteran is speaking up for Caitlin Clark, arguing the league is shooting itself in the foot by letting players rough her up without consequence. Clark has single-handedly moved the needle for women's basketball in a way nobody else has, and the concern is that the rough treatment could eventually wear on her. It's a fair point — you don't have the golden goose and then let everyone kick it.

stories.headline: Caleb Wilson Is Already Playing Chip-on-Shoulder Ball for the Bulls
stories.body: Nothing fuels a rookie like being passed over, and Caleb Wilson is fully locked in on proving the three guys drafted ahead of him wrong. He's already talking about being a game-changer in Chicago, which is exactly the kind of energy a Bulls rebuild needs. Draft slights have a funny way of producing very motivated basketball players.

stories.headline: Cubs Broom the Mets Right Out of Town
stories.body: Dansby Swanson and Pete Crow-Armstrong put on a show as Chicago swept New York in all four games this week — not exactly what the Mets needed right now. If you're a Cubs fan, this is the kind of week that makes you forget every bad April memory.

stories.headline: Mets Fire Mendoza, But the Real Problems Are Way Above His Pay Grade
stories.body: Carlos Mendoza is out as Mets manager, but let's be honest — you can't coach your way out of a 34-47 record when the roster itself is the issue. This feels less like a solution and more like ownership doing something visible so they don't have to do something meaningful.

stories.headline: Cooper Ingle Earns His Call-Up the Hard Way
stories.body: The Guardians called up Cooper Ingle this week, and his path to the show is a good one — he overhauled his nutrition and quietly put in the work between games until the organization had to notice. Cleveland's lineup has been struggling, so here's hoping the new guy brings some fresh energy.

stories.headline: Brendan Sorsby Has Now Been Rejected By Two Entire Leagues
stories.body: Texas Tech QB Brendan Sorsby, who admitted to gambling on his own team's games, has officially been shut out by both the NFL and the CFL — so that's every professional football league on the continent saying a hard pass. At some point the universe is sending a pretty clear message, Brendan. The door isn't just closed, it's locked, deadbolted, and someone slid a couch in front of it.

stories.headline: UVA's Athletic Department Has Some Explaining to Do
stories.body: Lacrosse coach Lars Tiffany's lawsuit against UVA is pulling back the curtain on some eyebrow-raising stuff about how the Cavaliers run their athletic department. Legal filings have a way of surfacing details that nobody in the administration wanted in the headlines, and this one seems to be doing exactly that. Grab some popcorn — this one could get messy before it gets better.

stories.headline: New Mexico State Is Ready to Show the World What Year Three Looks Like
stories.body: The Aggies are heading into their third season under Tony Sanchez, and someone went ahead and broke down every single opponent on their 2026 schedule so you don't have to. Year three is usually when a coach's system really starts to click, so New Mexico State fans might actually have some reasons to feel good heading into fall. Or at least reasons to look at the schedule and feel cautiously optimistic, which is basically the same thing.

stories.headline: Is the World Cup Slowly Killing the Game We Love?
stories.body: There's a growing chorus of voices worried that football is losing its soul to commercialization, expanding formats, and — yes — American influence. With the 2026 World Cup headed to North America, the hand-wringing is only going to get louder. Whether that's fair criticism or just old-school purists clutching their scarves is a debate worth having.

stories.headline: Alexi Lalas Is Terrible at His Job — And That's Exactly the Point
stories.body: Fox Sports' loudest American football pundit has been getting roasted by pretty much everyone with working ears during this World Cup, but here's the thing: Fox doesn't care, and neither does their target audience. Lalas isn't there to give you nuanced tactical breakdowns — he's there to be the guy at the bar who says something outrageous enough to keep you watching. Annoying? Sure. Effective? Apparently.

stories.headline: Spurs Do Spurs Things, Sign a Backup Keeper as Their Big Summer Move
stories.body: Tottenham have completed the signing of Martin Dubravka, which is essentially the club's way of telling Antonin Kinsky he's their guy in goal going forward. It's the fourth signing of Spurs' summer, which sounds productive until you remember they're still Spurs. Burnley, meanwhile, are closing in on Craig Bellamy — yes, that Craig Bellamy — in what is shaping up to be a busy window for the less glamorous clubs.

stories.headline: Le Mans Legend Ryo Hirakawa Gets His F1 Shot With Haas
stories.body: Endurance racing star Ryo Hirakawa — a Le Mans winner, no less — is stepping into Esteban Ocon's Haas seat for a Grand Prix outing in 2026. It's a cool crossover moment for a guy who's been absolutely dominant in long-distance racing, and honestly, if you can survive 24 hours at Le Mans, a Sunday afternoon in F1 probably feels like a quick errand.

stories.headline: Monaco's Drama Isn't Over — It's Headed to Court
stories.body: Just when you thought the Monaco GP was done and dusted, two teams are lawyering up and pushing to have the final result overturned. F1 is already rolling into Austria's stunning Styrian Mountains for this weekend's race, but the legal battle from the streets of Monte Carlo is very much still alive. Nothing like a courtroom subplot to keep the off-track entertainment going.

stories.headline: Electric Boats Are Giving F1 a Run for Its Money — On Water
stories.body: The E1 Series is quietly building serious buzz, bringing that slick, high-octane Formula 1 energy to open water with electric RaceBirds and destination events that look absolutely wild. It's still early days, but when F1 drivers and big-name stars start showing up to your races, you're clearly doing something right.

stories.headline: Wimbledon Champ Vondrousova Hit With Four-Year Ban in Stunning Fall From Grace
stories.body: Just three years after lifting the Wimbledon trophy, Marketa Vondrousova is now facing a four-year doping suspension after refusing a drug test back in December. She described herself as 'completely broken' by the news, and honestly, the tennis world is right there with her — nobody saw this one coming. It's a brutal turn for a player who was riding one of the sport's great Cinderella stories.

stories.headline: Columbus Is a Mess and Everyone Knows It
stories.body: Zach Werenski and Kirill Marchenko have both made it pretty clear they'd like out of Columbus, which is a rough look for a franchise still trying to convince people it's trending upward. The Blue Jackets did add a promising power forward in the first round, but it's hard to celebrate a new piece when two guys already in the building are quietly packing their bags.

stories.headline: Toronto Drafts Its Next Star — Now the Juggling Act Begins
stories.body: The Leafs snagged Gavin McKenna, and the fanbase is understandably hyped — the kid looks like a generational talent. But with Auston Matthews and Matthew Knies already locked into the core, Toronto's front office now has a very interesting puzzle to solve around the cap and the roster's long-term shape.

stories.headline: Pittsburgh Drafted One Ruck Twin — Now They've Got a Family Situation on Their Hands
stories.body: The Penguins took Liam Ruck in round one, which is great, but his identical twin Markus is still on the board heading into day two and you can bet Liam has some opinions about where his brother lands. The twins did a joint interview about it, because of course they did, and now the whole hockey world is rooting for Pittsburgh to just go ahead and make it a package deal.

stories.headline: Rory Gets a Rule Bended in His Favor, and Not Everyone's Happy About It
stories.body: The PGA Tour quietly invoked a commissioner's exemption to give Rory McIlroy relief from membership requirements amid his ongoing personal situation, and predictably, players noticed. Billy Horschel — never one to bite his tongue — was among the first to speak up, and a suspended tour pro also chimed in asking why the rulebook seems a little more flexible depending on who's holding the pen. It's the kind of inside-baseball tour politics that sounds boring until you realize it's basically about whether the stars get to play by different rules.

stories.headline: Ina Yoon Is Running Away With the KPMG and She Looks Ice Cold Doing It
stories.body: Yoon stretched her lead to five shots heading into the weekend at the KPMG Women's PGA Championship, leaving even Nelly Korda — the closest thing women's golf has to a sure thing — sitting six strokes back with serious ground to make up. Yoon's story has some layers to it, including a past ban for rules violations on the KLPGA, which makes her composure under a major spotlight all the more striking. Whether Korda can summon some weekend magic is the big question, but right now Yoon looks like she's playing a different game than everyone else.

stories.headline: Phil Mickelson Allegations Are Serious, and They're Not Going Away
stories.body: Journalist Alan Shipnuck — who literally wrote the book on Mickelson — is out with a detailed report alleging that Phil made unwanted sexual advances on multiple occasions, with the reporting backed by interviews with 19 people. The incidents described are lewd and serious, and this isn't coming out of nowhere given Mickelson's already complicated public legacy over the past decade. This one goes well beyond golf drama — it's a significant and troubling story about conduct that deserves more than a footnote.
